mnemonics: GIE (Global Interrupt Enable): Think of it as the master 'GO' switch for all interrupts. No GIE, no party.
keyPoints: Interrupts must be enabled globally (e.g., via a GIE bit in a status register) and individually for each specific interrupt source.
Disabling interrupts is often required to protect critical sections of code from being interrupted and corrupted.
The processor often automatically disables interrupts upon entering an ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) to prevent nested interrupts, unless explicitly re-enabled.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to re-enable interrupts after a critical section, causing the system to become unresponsive to future events.
Enabling a specific interrupt source without first enabling the global interrupt enable flag, rendering the interrupt inactive.
